lessons.title: Fundamental Concepts: Visual Angle, Near Point, Far Point and Simple Magnifier

content.body: Before studying complex optical instruments, we must understand the fundamental concepts that govern how the human eye perceives size and detail. Why does the moon appear small even though it is enormous? Why can we read fine print by holding a magnifying glass close to the eye? The answers lie in the visual angle — and understanding it is the key to understanding all optical instruments from the simple magnifying glass to the powerful compound microscope used in Ugandan medical laboratories.
The Human Eye: Near Point, Far Point and Accommodation

**Near point (D):** The closest point at which the normal eye can see an object clearly without strain. For a normal eye: D = 25 cm. (The eye's lens is at maximum curvature.)

**Far point:** The farthest point at which the normal eye can see clearly. For a normal eye: far point = infinity. (The eye's lens is at minimum curvature — relaxed.)

**Accommodation:** The ability of the eye lens to change its focal length (by changing curvature using the ciliary muscles) to focus on objects at different distances. Range of accommodation = near point to far point.

**Normal vision:** Near point = 25 cm; Far point = ∞
**Myopic (short-sighted):** Near point closer than 25 cm; Far point closer than ∞
**Hypermetropic (long-sighted):** Near point farther than 25 cm
**Presbyopic (age-related):** Near point recedes with age — over 45, near point may be 50 cm or more

**Exit pupil (eye ring):** The image of the objective lens formed by the eyepiece — the optimal position for the observer's eye. For maximum field of view and minimum eye strain, the observer's eye should be at the exit pupil.
Visual Angle and Angular Magnification

**Visual angle (θ):** The angle subtended by an object at the eye (or at the optical centre of the eye lens). The apparent size of an object depends on the visual angle — a large object far away can subtend the same visual angle as a small object close up.

For object of height h at distance d: **θ ≈ h/d** (in radians, for small angles)

**Magnifying power (angular magnification, M):** The ratio of the visual angle subtended by the image (as seen through the instrument) to the visual angle subtended by the object when placed at the near point (25 cm) without the instrument.

$$M = \frac{\text{angle subtended by image through instrument}}{\text{angle subtended by object at near point (25 cm)}}$$

$$M = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}$$

**Simple magnifier (magnifying glass) — single converging lens:**

An object placed just inside the focal point of a converging lens produces a virtual, erect, magnified image.

**Case 1: Image at near point (D = 25 cm) — normal use:**

The image is at 25 cm from the eye. Object is at u from the lens.
1/v + 1/u = 1/f → since image is virtual at D: v = −D = −25 cm
1/u = 1/f + 1/25

Magnifying power: M = D/u = D(1/f + 1/D) = **D/f + 1**

$$\boxed{M = \frac{D}{f} + 1 \quad \text{(image at near point D)}}$$

**Case 2: Image at infinity — relaxed eye:**

Object is placed at the focal point F.

$$\boxed{M = \frac{D}{f} \quad \text{(image at infinity, relaxed eye)}}$$

**Worked Example 1:** A converging lens of focal length 5 cm is used as a magnifying glass. Find the magnifying power when the image is at (a) 25 cm and (b) infinity. (D = 25 cm)

(a) M = D/f + 1 = 25/5 + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6×
(b) M = D/f = 25/5 = 5×

**Worked Example 2:** A magnifying glass gives a magnification of 8× with the image at infinity. What is the focal length?

M = D/f: 8 = 25/f → f = 25/8 = 3.125 cm
The Eye Ring (Exit Pupil)

**Eye ring (exit pupil):** In any optical instrument with an eyepiece, the image of the objective (or entry pupil) formed by the eyepiece is called the eye ring or exit pupil. This is the optimal position for the observer's pupil:

- All the light that has passed through the instrument exits through this small circle
- Placing the eye here maximises the field of view
- Placing the eye closer or further from the eyepiece reduces the field of view

**Location of exit pupil:**
The exit pupil is located at a distance from the eyepiece equal to the image distance when the objective is treated as an object for the eyepiece. For instruments in normal adjustment (final image at infinity), the exit pupil is at the back focal point of the eyepiece.

**Exit pupil size:** Diameter of exit pupil = Diameter of objective / Magnification

For a telescope with 80 mm objective and magnification 16×:
Exit pupil = 80/16 = 5 mm (approximately matches the human pupil in moderate light)

**Significance:** If the exit pupil is smaller than the eye's pupil, all collected light enters the eye — maximum brightness. If larger, some light is wasted. For astronomical telescopes used at night, a 5–7 mm exit pupil is ideal (matches the dark-adapted pupil).

content.title: Summary
content.body: Near point D = 25 cm (normal eye). Far point = ∞. Accommodation: eye adjusts focus. Visual angle θ = h/d. Magnifying power M = β/α. Simple magnifier: M = D/f+1 (image at D); M = D/f (image at ∞). Eye ring = exit pupil = optimal eye position.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Near point (D): Closest distance for clear vision without strain; D = 25 cm for normal eye
• Far point: Farthest distance for clear vision; infinity for normal eye
• Visual angle: Angle subtended by object at the eye; determines apparent size: θ ≈ h/d
• Magnifying power (M): Ratio of visual angle through instrument to visual angle at near point without instrument
• Exit pupil (eye ring): Image of objective formed by eyepiece; optimal position for observer's eye

questions.question: The near point of a normal human eye is approximately:
questions.explanation: The near point D of the normal eye is 25 cm — the closest distance at which the eye can focus without strain. This value is used in all magnifying power calculations.

questions.question: A magnifying glass has focal length 5 cm. What is its magnifying power when the image is at infinity? (D = 25 cm)
questions.explanation: M = D/f = 25/5 = 5× (image at infinity — relaxed eye). If image at near point: M = D/f + 1 = 6×.

questions.question: An astronomical telescope has objective f_o = 80 cm and eyepiece f_e = 4 cm. What is the magnification in normal adjustment?
questions.explanation: M = f_o/f_e = 80/4 = 20×. The image is inverted in normal adjustment.

questions.question: The length of an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment (objective f_o = 90 cm, eyepiece f_e = 5 cm) is:
questions.explanation: Length in normal adjustment = f_o + f_e = 90 + 5 = 95 cm. (The two focal points coincide — objective's back focal point and eyepiece's front focal point.)

questions.question: In a compound microscope, which lens is placed closest to the specimen?
questions.explanation: The objective lens (short focal length, typically 1–5 mm) is placed very close to the specimen to collect and magnify it into a real intermediate image. The eyepiece then further magnifies this intermediate image.

questions.question: A compound microscope has f_o = 0.5 cm, f_e = 2.5 cm, and tube length L = 15 cm. What is the magnification in normal adjustment? (D = 25 cm)
A telescope magnifies 40× in normal adjustment. If f_e = 2.5 cm, what is f_o?
In a compound microscope, the intermediate image formed by the objective is:
questions.explanation: The objective lens in a microscope forms a real, inverted, magnified intermediate image of the specimen. The object is placed just beyond the focal point of the objective (u slightly > f_o).

questions.question: A Galilean telescope uses a diverging eyepiece. How does its image differ from the astronomical telescope?
options: The Galilean telescope produces an inverted image; astronomical produces erect
The Galilean telescope produces an erect image; astronomical produces inverted
Both produce the same type of image
The Galilean telescope produces a real image; astronomical produces virtual
questions.answer: The Galilean telescope produces an erect image; astronomical produces inverted
questions.explanation: The Galilean telescope uses a diverging (concave) lens as the eyepiece, which intercepts the converging rays from the objective before they form an intermediate image. This produces an erect (upright) final image — suitable for terrestrial viewing.

questions.question: A magnifying glass gives M = 6× with image at near point (D=25 cm). What is the focal length?
An astronomical telescope has f_o = 60 cm and f_e = 3 cm. Find the magnification when the final image is at D = 25 cm.

content.body: A compound microscope is one of the most important instruments in biological and medical science. At Mulago National Referral Hospital in Kampala, compound microscopes are used daily to diagnose malaria, tuberculosis, and other diseases by examining blood smears and tissue samples. The compound microscope achieves magnifications of 100× to 2000× by combining two converging lenses — an objective and an eyepiece.
Construction and Ray Diagram

**The compound microscope** consists of two converging lenses:

1. **Objective lens:** Short focal length (f_o, typically 1–5 mm). Positioned close to the specimen. Forms a real, inverted, magnified intermediate image of the object.

2. **Eyepiece (ocular):** Longer focal length (f_e, typically 25–50 mm). Acts as a simple magnifier to view the intermediate image.

**Setup:**
- Object is placed just outside the focal point of the objective (u_o slightly > f_o)
- The objective forms a real, inverted, magnified image (intermediate image) inside the microscope tube
- This intermediate image lies just inside the focal point of the eyepiece (within f_e of the eyepiece)
- The eyepiece magnifies this intermediate image to produce the final image seen by the observer

**Ray diagram key features:**
- Two rays drawn from object tip: one parallel to axis (→ through F_o after objective), one through optical centre (undeviated)
- These define the intermediate image (real, inverted, magnified — between the two lenses)
- Two rays from intermediate image tip through eyepiece: one through F_e (→ parallel after eyepiece), one through optical centre
- These produce a virtual, inverted (relative to object), further magnified final image

**Tube length (L):** The distance between the back focal point of the objective and the front focal point of the eyepiece (the distance available for forming the intermediate image inside the tube). Typically L ≈ 150–170 mm for standard microscopes.
Magnification: Normal and Abnormal Adjustment

**Normal adjustment (final image at infinity):**
The eye is relaxed. The intermediate image falls exactly at the focal point of the eyepiece.

Magnification of objective: m_o = v_o/u_o ≈ L/f_o (for large magnification)

Magnification of eyepiece: m_e = D/f_e (simple magnifier with image at ∞)

**Total magnification (normal adjustment):**
$$\boxed{M = m_o \times m_e = \frac{L}{f_o} \times \frac{D}{f_e}}$$

**Abnormal adjustment (final image at near point D = 25 cm):**
The eye is strained but magnification is maximum. The intermediate image falls slightly inside the focal point of the eyepiece.

Magnification of eyepiece: m_e = D/f_e + 1

**Total magnification (abnormal adjustment):**
$$M = \frac{v_o}{u_o} \times \left(\frac{D}{f_e} + 1\right)$$

For large magnification: M ≈ (L/f_o) × (D/f_e + 1)

**Worked Example 1:** A compound microscope has objective focal length 0.8 cm and eyepiece focal length 2.5 cm. The tube length L = 16 cm. D = 25 cm. Find the magnifying power in (a) normal and (b) abnormal adjustment.

(a) Normal: M = (L/f_o) × (D/f_e) = (16/0.8) × (25/2.5) = 20 × 10 = 200×

(b) Abnormal: M = (L/f_o) × (D/f_e + 1) = 20 × (10 + 1) = 20 × 11 = 220×

**Worked Example 2:** A microscope gives magnification 400× in normal adjustment with eyepiece f_e = 5 cm (D=25 cm). What is the objective's magnification?

M_eyepiece = D/f_e = 25/5 = 5×
M_objective = M_total/M_eyepiece = 400/5 = 80×

**Worked Example 3:** An object is 0.9 cm from an objective lens of f_o = 0.8 cm. The eyepiece has f_e = 3 cm. D=25 cm. Find M in normal adjustment.

For objective: 1/v_o = 1/f_o − 1/u_o = 1/0.8 − 1/0.9 = (0.9−0.8)/(0.8×0.9) = 0.1/0.72
v_o = 7.2 cm
m_o = v_o/u_o = 7.2/0.9 = 8
M_total = m_o × D/f_e = 8 × 25/3 = 8 × 8.33 = 66.7×
Applications of the Compound Microscope in Uganda

The compound microscope is indispensable in Ugandan healthcare and research:

**1. Medical diagnosis:**
- **Malaria:** Examination of thin/thick blood smears stained with Giemsa — Plasmodium parasites visible inside red blood cells (×400–1000 magnification)
- **Tuberculosis (TB):** Sputum smear microscopy — Ziehl-Neelsen stained smears detect Mycobacterium tuberculosis (×1000 with oil immersion)
- **HIV:** CD4 cell counting; monitoring opportunistic infections
- **Sexually transmitted infections:** Microscopy of discharge samples
- **Urine microscopy:** Detecting bacteria, blood cells, and casts in urine

**2. Pathology:**
Examination of biopsied tissue sections to diagnose cancer (e.g., cervical cancer screening at Mulago), inflammatory conditions, and parasitic infections.

**3. Agricultural research:**
At National Agricultural Research Organisation (NARO) facilities, microscopes examine soil microorganisms, plant pathogens (fungal spores, bacteria), insect anatomy, and seed quality.

**4. Environmental monitoring:**
Examination of water samples for pathogenic microorganisms (Giardia, Cryptosporidium) — important for monitoring Lake Victoria and other water sources.

**5. Education:**
In Ugandan university and O-Level laboratories, microscopes allow students to observe cells, bacteria, pond life, and tissue structure firsthand.

**Magnification ranges:**
| Objective | Eyepiece | Total magnification |
|---|---|---|
| 4× | 10× | 40× |
| 10× | 10× | 100× |
| 40× | 10× | 400× |
| 100× (oil) | 10× | 1000× |

The 100× oil immersion objective is used to diagnose malaria and TB — at this magnification, cells of about 1 μm can be resolved.

content.title: Summary
content.body: Compound microscope: objective (short f_o) + eyepiece (longer f_e). Objective forms real magnified intermediate image; eyepiece acts as magnifier. Normal adjustment (relaxed eye): M = (L/f_o)×(D/f_e). Abnormal (eye strained): M = (v_o/u_o)×(D/f_e+1). Applications: malaria/TB diagnosis, pathology, agricultural research.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Objective lens: Short-focal-length lens in a microscope closest to the specimen; forms real magnified intermediate image
• Eyepiece (ocular): Longer-focal-length lens in a microscope used to view the intermediate image as a simple magnifier
• Normal adjustment: Final image at infinity; relaxed eye; M = (L/f_o)×(D/f_e)
• Tube length (L): Distance between back focal point of objective and front focal point of eyepiece

questions.question: A microscope objective (f_o = 0.8 cm) forms an image when object is at 0.9 cm. Eyepiece f_e = 4 cm. Find total magnification in normal adjustment. (D=25 cm)
A compound microscope gives 400× magnification in normal adjustment. Objective magnification is 40×. What is the eyepiece focal length? (D=25 cm)
The magnifying power of a compound microscope in normal adjustment is given by:
questions.explanation: Normal adjustment: M = m_o × m_e = (L/f_o) × (D/f_e). The objective magnifies by L/f_o (tube length/objective focal length) and the eyepiece acts as a simple magnifier D/f_e.

questions.question: Why does a telescope require a large-diameter objective lens?
options: A larger objective gives higher magnification
A larger objective collects more light (light gathering ∝ D²) allowing fainter objects to be seen and providing better resolution
A larger objective reduces chromatic aberration
A larger objective allows a wider field of view with the eyepiece
questions.answer: A larger objective collects more light (light gathering ∝ D²) allowing fainter objects to be seen and providing better resolution
questions.explanation: Light gathering power ∝ area of objective ∝ D². A larger objective captures more photons from faint, distant objects (stars, planets). It also improves resolving power (ability to separate fine detail) since the Rayleigh limit θ = 1.22λ/D decreases with larger D.

questions.question: In a compound microscope in normal adjustment, the intermediate image is located:
options: At the focal point of the objective
At the focal point of the eyepiece (inside the eyepiece)
Between the objective's back focal point and the eyepiece's front focal point (in the tube)
questions.answer: Between the objective's back focal point and the eyepiece's front focal point (in the tube)
questions.explanation: The intermediate image forms inside the microscope tube — beyond the objective's back focal point but exactly at the eyepiece's front focal point (for normal adjustment). This allows the eyepiece to form the final image at infinity.

questions.question: The image formed by an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment is:
options: Real, inverted, at the near point
Virtual, erect, at the far point
questions.explanation: In normal adjustment, the astronomical telescope produces a virtual, inverted image at infinity. Virtual because the eyepiece diverges the rays; inverted because both lenses invert once — the two inversions don't cancel for the telescope geometry.

questions.question: Which correctly explains why malaria can be diagnosed using a compound microscope?
options: The microscope produces coloured images to distinguish different cells
At 400–1000× magnification, Plasmodium parasites (1–10 μm) inside red blood cells can be seen and identified after Giemsa staining
The microscope's light kills the malaria parasites, confirming their presence
Malaria causes cells to appear larger, visible even at 10×
questions.answer: At 400–1000× magnification, Plasmodium parasites (1–10 μm) inside red blood cells can be seen and identified after Giemsa staining
questions.explanation: Malaria parasites (Plasmodium species) are 1–10 micrometres in size — invisible to the naked eye. At 400–1000× magnification with Giemsa or Field's stain, the ring-stage trophozoites and other forms inside red blood cells are clearly visible, allowing species identification.

questions.question: A Galilean telescope (diverging eyepiece f_e = −4 cm) and objective f_o = 60 cm. What is the magnification and tube length?
questions.explanation: M = f_o/|f_e| = 60/4 = 15×. Length = f_o − |f_e| = 60 − 4 = 56 cm. The Galilean telescope is shorter than the astronomical telescope with the same magnification because the eyepiece intercepts rays before they form the intermediate image.

questions.question: In a compound microscope, the eyepiece acts as:
options: A projector, forming a real image on a screen
A simple magnifier (magnifying glass) for the intermediate image
A diverging lens to spread the light uniformly
A mirror to redirect the image toward the eye
questions.answer: A simple magnifier (magnifying glass) for the intermediate image
questions.explanation: The eyepiece functions as a simple magnifier (magnifying glass) for the real intermediate image formed by the objective. It produces a virtual, further magnified image at infinity (normal adjustment) or at the near point (abnormal adjustment).

questions.question: In normal adjustment of a telescope, where does the intermediate image formed by the objective fall relative to the eyepiece?
options: At the optical centre of the eyepiece
At the front focal point of the eyepiece (F_e)
Beyond the back focal point of the eyepiece
Between the two lenses but not at any focal point
questions.answer: At the front focal point of the eyepiece (F_e)
questions.explanation: In normal adjustment (final image at ∞), the intermediate image must be at the front focal point of the eyepiece (F_e). This makes the eyepiece produce parallel rays — so the final image is at infinity and the observer's eye is relaxed.

questions.question: A microscope objective has focal length 1.0 cm and an object is placed 1.1 cm from it. What is the objective magnification?

content.body: A refracting telescope uses lenses to collect and magnify light from distant objects — stars, planets, distant mountains, or ships at sea. Unlike the microscope which magnifies very close, small objects, the telescope magnifies distant, large objects. The principle was first used by Galileo in 1609, and refracting telescopes are still used today in navigation, astronomy, and military applications across East Africa.
Construction and Ray Diagram of the Astronomical Telescope

**The astronomical (refracting) telescope** consists of two converging lenses:

1. **Objective lens:** Large diameter, long focal length (f_o). Collects light from the distant object.
2. **Eyepiece:** Short focal length (f_e). Magnifies the image formed by the objective.

**How it works — Normal adjustment (final image at infinity):**
1. Distant object subtends a small angle α at the objective
2. The objective forms a real, inverted image at its focal plane (at distance f_o from objective, since object is at ∞)
3. This image lies at the focal point F_o of the objective AND at the focal point F_e of the eyepiece (the two focal points coincide for normal adjustment)
4. The eyepiece refracts the diverging rays from this image into a parallel beam
5. The observer's relaxed eye sees a magnified, virtual image at infinity

**Total length of telescope in normal adjustment:**
L = f_o + f_e (objective focal length + eyepiece focal length)

**Ray diagram construction:**
- Two parallel rays from tip of distant object strike objective
- After objective: converge to intermediate image at focal plane (f_o from objective)
- Rays diverge from this image, hit the eyepiece
- Eyepiece (object at F_e) refracts them into parallel beam
- Final image at infinity (eye relaxed)

**The intermediate image is:**
- Real, inverted
- Located at f_o from objective = f_e from eyepiece
- Same size as the image formed by objective alone
Magnification: Normal and Abnormal Adjustment

**Magnifying power in normal adjustment (final image at infinity):**

The visual angle without telescope: α = h/f_o (where h = size of intermediate image)
The visual angle with telescope: β = h/f_e

$$\boxed{M = \frac{\beta}{\alpha} = \frac{h/f_e}{h/f_o} = \frac{f_o}{f_e}}$$

**Magnification of astronomical telescope (normal adjustment) = f_o/f_e**

The image is inverted (this is acceptable for astronomical observations but not for terrestrial use).

**Abnormal adjustment (final image at near point D):**
The eyepiece is moved closer to the objective, so the intermediate image falls inside the focal length of the eyepiece, forming a virtual image at D.

For the eyepiece as a simple magnifier with image at D:
m_e = D/f_e + 1

$$\boxed{M = \frac{f_o}{f_e}\left(1 + \frac{f_e}{D}\right) = \frac{f_o}{f_e} + \frac{f_o}{D}}$$

For D = 25 cm: M = f_o/f_e + f_o/25

Alternatively: M = f_o(1/f_e + 1/D) or M = (f_o/f_e)(1 + f_e/D)

**Note:** Abnormal adjustment gives slightly higher magnification than normal adjustment.

**Worked Example 1:** An astronomical telescope has objective f_o = 100 cm and eyepiece f_e = 5 cm. Find:
(a) Magnification in normal adjustment
(b) Length of telescope in normal adjustment
(c) Magnification in abnormal adjustment (D=25 cm)

(a) M = f_o/f_e = 100/5 = 20×
(b) L = f_o + f_e = 100 + 5 = 105 cm
(c) M = f_o/f_e + f_o/D = 100/5 + 100/25 = 20 + 4 = 24×

**Worked Example 2:** A telescope magnifies 30× in normal adjustment. The eyepiece has f_e = 4 cm. Find the objective focal length and the telescope length.

M = f_o/f_e: f_o = M × f_e = 30 × 4 = 120 cm
L = f_o + f_e = 120 + 4 = 124 cm

**Worked Example 3:** A telescope objective (f_o = 75 cm) and eyepiece (f_e = 3 cm). Find M at near point D = 25 cm.

M = f_o/f_e + f_o/D = 75/3 + 75/25 = 25 + 3 = 28×

**Comparing Normal vs Abnormal adjustment:**
| Feature | Normal (image at ∞) | Abnormal (image at D) |
|---|---|---|
| Eye | Relaxed | Strained |
| Magnification | f_o/f_e | f_o/f_e + f_o/D |
| Tube length | f_o + f_e | f_o + f_e − x (eyepiece moved in) |
| Preferred for | Extended observation | Maximum magnification |
The Terrestrial Telescope and Applications

**The astronomical telescope** produces an inverted image — acceptable for viewing stars but not for terrestrial (land) observations where an upright image is needed.

**Galilean telescope:**
Uses a diverging lens as the eyepiece — produces an erect image.
- M = f_o/f_e (f_e is the focal length of the diverging eyepiece, taken as positive value)
- Length = f_o − f_e (shorter than astronomical)
- Field of view narrower than equivalent astronomical telescope
- Used in: opera glasses, some binoculars, Galileo's original telescope

**Terrestrial (erecting) telescope:**
An astronomical telescope with an additional erecting lens between objective and eyepiece to invert the image. Used in military periscopes and land-survey telescopes (theodolites).

**Applications of telescopes:**

**1. Astronomy:**
Viewing planets, the Moon, star clusters, and nebulae. Ugandan students at schools near dark-sky sites (e.g., Bwindi, Kidepo) can use small refracting telescopes to observe the Moon and Jupiter's moons.

**2. Navigation:**
Ship navigation on Lake Victoria — officers use telescopes to identify buoys, lighthouses, and other vessels. Uganda Marine (boat police) uses binoculars based on the same principles.

**3. Military/security:**
Binoculars use two astronomical telescope systems (with inverting prisms for erect image) for surveillance. Uganda People's Defence Force (UPDF) uses these for field operations.

**4. Land surveying:**
The theodolite (surveying instrument) contains a small telescope used to measure angles for construction and mapping. Used by Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) for road design.

**5. Bird watching and wildlife observation:**
Uganda is one of the world's top birding destinations (over 1000 species). Binoculars/telescopes are essential for observing birds at Bwindi, Queen Elizabeth National Park, and Murchison Falls.

**Why large objectives are better:**
- Larger aperture → more light collected → fainter objects visible (light-gathering power ∝ D²)
- Larger aperture → better resolution (ability to distinguish fine detail — Rayleigh criterion)
- Higher possible magnification (though atmospheric turbulence limits practical magnification)

content.title: Summary
content.body: Refracting telescope: large-aperture long-f_o objective + short-f_e eyepiece. Normal adjustment: M = f_o/f_e; L = f_o + f_e; image inverted, at ∞. Abnormal: M = f_o/f_e + f_o/D. Galilean telescope: diverging eyepiece → erect image. Applications: astronomy, navigation, surveying, military, wildlife.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Objective (telescope): Large-aperture, long-focal-length converging lens that collects light from distant objects
• Normal adjustment (telescope): Final image at infinity; relaxed eye; M = f_o/f_e; length = f_o + f_e
• Galilean telescope: Telescope with diverging eyepiece producing erect image; M = f_o/f_e; length = f_o − |f_e|
• Resolving power: Ability to distinguish two closely spaced objects as separate; improves with larger objective aperture

questions.question: A compound microscope in abnormal adjustment gives M = 330×. Objective magnification = 30×. f_e = ? (D=25 cm)
An astronomical telescope is used for bird watching but gives an inverted image. What is the simplest modification?
options: Replace the objective with a concave mirror
Add an erecting lens between objective and eyepiece, or replace eyepiece with a diverging lens (Galilean type)
View through the eyepiece from the other end
Use a shorter eyepiece focal length
questions.answer: Add an erecting lens between objective and eyepiece, or replace eyepiece with a diverging lens (Galilean type)
questions.explanation: An astronomical telescope gives an inverted image. For terrestrial use, either: (1) add an erecting lens (makes a terrestrial telescope — 4 lenses total), or (2) use a Galilean-type diverging eyepiece (naturally gives erect image). Modern binoculars use Porro prisms to erect the image.

questions.question: The exit pupil (eye ring) of a telescope is:
options: The pupil of the observer's eye
The image of the objective formed by the eyepiece — the optimal position for the observer's eye
The lens closest to the observer's eye
The focal point of the eyepiece
questions.answer: The image of the objective formed by the eyepiece — the optimal position for the observer's eye
questions.explanation: The exit pupil (eye ring) is the image of the objective aperture formed by the eyepiece. Placing the eye here maximises the field of view and ensures all light collected by the objective enters the eye. Exit pupil diameter = objective diameter / magnification.

questions.question: A refracting telescope in normal adjustment has length 90 cm. If f_e = 6 cm, what is M?
Which adjustment gives greater magnification for an astronomical telescope — normal or abnormal — and why?
options: Normal: M = f_o/f_e; abnormal is always smaller
Abnormal: M = f_o/f_e + f_o/D > f_o/f_e (since f_o/D > 0)
It depends on the distance to the object
questions.answer: Abnormal: M = f_o/f_e + f_o/D > f_o/f_e (since f_o/D > 0)
questions.explanation: Abnormal M = f_o/f_e + f_o/D. Normal M = f_o/f_e. Since f_o/D > 0 always, abnormal M > normal M. However, abnormal adjustment strains the eye — the extra magnification f_o/D is usually small compared to f_o/f_e.

questions.question: The visual angle is important in optical instruments because:
options: It determines the colour of the image
The apparent size of an object depends only on the visual angle subtended at the eye — the magnifier increases this angle
It determines the focal length needed for a given magnification
Objects at the visual angle appear sharper than others
questions.answer: The apparent size of an object depends only on the visual angle subtended at the eye — the magnifier increases this angle
questions.explanation: The apparent size of any object depends entirely on the visual angle it subtends at the eye — not on the actual size. An optical instrument works by increasing this angle compared to viewing the unaided object at the near point. Magnifying power = ratio of angles.

questions.question: Which of the following is a real-life application of the refracting telescope in Uganda?
options: Examining blood slides in Mulago Hospital
Uganda Marine Police using binoculars (based on telescope principles) to monitor Lake Victoria
questions.answer: Uganda Marine Police using binoculars (based on telescope principles) to monitor Lake Victoria
questions.explanation: Binoculars use two astronomical telescope systems with Porro prisms for erect images. Uganda Marine Police and Uganda Wildlife Authority rangers use binoculars based on refracting telescope principles for monitoring Lake Victoria and national parks. Blood slides use compound microscopes (LO1).

questions.question: A compound microscope has M = 500× in abnormal adjustment, objective magnification = 40×. What is the eyepiece focal length if image is at D=25 cm?
For maximum magnification with a refracting telescope, which should be true about the focal lengths?
options: f_o should be short and f_e should be long
f_o should be long and f_e should be short
f_o and f_e should be equal
Both should be as short as possible
questions.answer: f_o should be long and f_e should be short
questions.explanation: M = f_o/f_e. For maximum M: maximize f_o and minimize f_e. Long-focal-length objectives and short-focal-length eyepieces give maximum magnification. However, longer f_o means a physically longer telescope.

questions.question: In normal adjustment of a compound microscope, the final image is at:
options: The near point (25 cm from eye)
The focal point of the objective
The focal point of the eyepiece
questions.explanation: In normal adjustment, the intermediate image is at the front focal point of the eyepiece, so the eyepiece produces parallel rays and the final image is at infinity — the eye is relaxed. This is preferred for extended observations to reduce eye fatigue.

questions.question: A telescope objective has diameter 80 mm and gives M = 20× in normal adjustment. What is the exit pupil diameter?
questions.explanation: Exit pupil diameter = objective diameter / magnification = 80/20 = 4.0 mm. This is close to the eye's pupil size in moderate light (~3–5 mm), ensuring all collected light enters the eye efficiently.
